summary: sensivity ships a Windows backdoor: HKCU Run-key persistence impersonating OneDrive, eval'd obfuscated server, and undisclosed native binary with game-cheat artifacts
details: The package installs persistent autostart on Windows by writing an HKCU\Software\Microsoft\Windows\CurrentVersion\Run entry named 'OneDriveUpdate' that points at a bundled OneDrive.Standalone.Updater.vbs (launcher.js:80). The VBS silently invokes `node launcher.js` with a hidden window (OneDrive.Standalone.Updater.vbs:7). launcher.js then forks a detached supervisor that respawns a worker forever, writes a `.sensivity-supervisor.pid` dotfile, and binds a control panel on TCP 3000. The launcher renames the supervisor and worker to `process.title = 'Runtime Broker'` (impersonating the real Windows DCOM process) and monkey-patches console.log/warn/error to redact every mention of `sens.node` (replacing it with 'program module') and to swallow the 'program module loaded' line — active anti-analysis to hide the loaded native addon from anyone tailing output. The launcher terminates by reading server.obf.js (135 KB obfuscator.io output, 646-entry string array) and passing the whole text to `eval` (launcher.js:295), executing opaque code in-process alongside the persistence and native-addon logic. The bundled `sens.node` is a 6.8 MB Windows PE whose strings include 'Teleport', 'LeftFoot', 'LeftHand', 'Head', 'Settings', and '__licenseAccepted' — characteristic of a Roblox-style aim/ESP/teleport cheat — while the package self-describes only as a 'Sensivity Control Panel'. A monitor loop polls chrome/msedge/opera/opera_gx/brave every 3 s for a 'YouTube' MainWindowTitle and only surfaces the QR/control UI when a YouTube tab is foreground, writing throwaway `.yt.ps1` / `.qrshow.ps1` PowerShell helpers — a streamer-bait evasion pattern that hides the control surface from casual inspection. Microsoft brand names (OneDrive, Runtime Broker, 'Windows PowerShell' window title) are reused throughout to deceive users reviewing Task Manager, autoruns, or Run keys. Installing or invoking this package on Windows establishes a persistent, cloaked backdoor with an opaque native payload on the developer's machine.

cwes.description: The product contains code that appears to be malicious in nature.
cwes.name: Embedded Malicious Code
